evidence_text: The method scores incoming knowledge objects using source reputation, novelty, and source reliability, without oracle access to ground-truth quality labels.
source_excerpt:
“We introduce write-time gating that filters incoming knowledge objects using composite salience scores (source reputation, novelty, reliability)” and “Our three signals—reputation, novelty, and source reliability—are all observable at write time from metadata alone, without knowing whether the candidate fact is true.”
```

evidence_text: Objects below the salience threshold are archived in cold storage rather than deleted.
source_excerpt:
“Objects with S(K) < τ enter the archive rather than being deleted.” The paper says this preserves future promotion, audit trails, and negative evidence about rejected content.
```

evidence_text: When new content updates a concept, the system links old and new objects through supersession links instead of overwriting.
source_excerpt:
“The system records bidirectional links: K_old.superseded_by ← K_new.id and K_new.supersedes ← K_old.id. The old object moves to the archive while the new object enters the active store.”
```

evidence_text: The main synthetic benchmark used 50 knowledge objects: 10 correct facts and 40 distractors.
source_excerpt:
“The benchmark contains 50 knowledge objects: 10 correct facts from high-reputation sources... and 40 distractors from low-reputation sources.”
```

evidence_text: In the ungated condition on the synthetic benchmark, accuracy was 13.3% ± 4.7%.
source_excerpt:
“Distractors constitute 80% of stored content... achieving only 13.3% ± 4.7% accuracy.” Table 2 lists “Ungated... Accuracy 13.3% ± 4.7%.”
```

evidence_text: With write gating on the synthetic benchmark, 12–14 objects were admitted and accuracy reached 100% ± 0%.
source_excerpt:
“With reputation-based write gating, only 12–14 objects pass the salience filter (72–76% compression).” The same section states, “accuracy reaches 100% ± 0% across all seeds.”
```

evidence_text: In the temporal-query experiment, the archive-with-lineage system achieved 100% accuracy and the overwrite system achieved 0% accuracy on prior-state queries.
source_excerpt:
“The archive-with-lineage system maintains supersession chains and achieves 100% accuracy on temporal queries... The overwrite system replaces prior versions and achieves 0% accuracy on queries about prior states.”
```

evidence_text: On the synthetic read-time filtering comparison, write gating reached 100.0% ± 0.0%, Self-RAG reached 93.8% ± 6.3%, and combining write gating with Self-RAG also yielded 93.8% ± 6.3%.
source_excerpt: Table 3 reports: “Self-RAG (read-time) 93.8% ± 6.3%,” “Write gating (write-time) 100% ± 0.0%,” and “Both (write + read) 93.8% ± 6.3%.” The text adds that “Adding read-time filtering to an already-clean store degrades performance because the Self-RAG critic occasionally rejects correct facts.”
note:
The source presents the combined condition as worse than write gating alone because the critic introduces false negatives in an already-clean store.
```
caveat:
The source presents the combined condition as worse than write gating alone because the critic introduces false negatives in an already-clean store.
```

evidence_text: At an 8:1 distractor ratio in the synthetic scaling experiment, ungated retrieval scored 0%, Self-RAG scored 0%, and write gating scored 100%.
On Wikipedia facts at a 4:1 distractor ratio, write gating achieved 98.1% ± 1.1% accuracy versus 85.2% ± 2.0% for ungated retrieval.
source_excerpt:
Table 5 reports “Ungated 85.2% ± 2.0%” and “Write-gated 98.1% ± 1.1%.” The text says, “Write gating achieves 98.1% accuracy versus 85.2% ungated.”
```

evidence_text: On Wikipedia at 4:1, removing the source label signal reduced write-gated accuracy from 97.8% ± 1.1% to 96.4% ± 1.4%.
source_excerpt:
Table 8 lists “Full gating (rep + nov + label) 97.8% ± 1.1%” and “No label (rep + novelty only) 96.4% ± 1.4%.” The text says, “Removing the source label signal reduces accuracy by only 1.4 percentage points.”
```

evidence_text: On procedural pharmacology data at 8:1, ungated retrieval scored 32.0% ± 4.0%, write gating scored 96.6% ± 1.6%, and Self-RAG scored 80.2% ± 2.1%.
On 2026 arXiv papers at 8:1, ungated retrieval scored 45.2% ± 2.0%, write gating scored 93.6% ± 3.2%, and Self-RAG scored 83.2% ± 1.6%.

The paper states that with k=8 retrieved passages, Self-RAG uses 9 LLM calls per query versus 1 for write gating, and it also states that its evaluation uses limited sample sizes and a Self-RAG-style prompted critic rather than the trained Self-RAG model.
source_excerpt: “In our experiments with k = 8 retrieved passages, Self-RAG uses 9 LLM calls per query versus 1 for write gating.” In limitations, the paper says, “Experiments 1 and 2 use 3 and 2 random seeds respectively,” “Experiment 3 (distractor scaling) reports single-seed results per ratio,” and “the read-time filtering baseline uses a zero-shot LLM critic rather than the trained Self-RAG model.”
note: This unit combines one concrete cost claim with explicit evaluation caveats that bound how broadly the reported comparison should be interpreted.
caveat: This unit combines one concrete cost claim with explicit evaluation caveats that bound how broadly the reported comparison should be interpreted.
